bibliographic_data.title: Toward Scalable and Controllable AR Experimentation
bibliographic_data.abstract: To understand how well a proposed augmented reality (AR) solution works, existing papers often conducted tailored and isolated evaluations for specific AR tasks, e.g., depth or lighting estimation, and compared them to easy-to-setup baselines, either using datasets or resorting to time-consuming data capturing. Conceptually simple, it can be extremely difficult to evaluate an AR system fairly and in scale to understand its real-world performance. The difficulties arise for three key reasons: lack of control of the physical environment, the time-consuming data capturing, and the difficulties to reproduce baseline results. This paper presents our design of an AR experimentation platform, ExpAR, aiming to provide scalable and controllable AR experimentation. ExpAR is envisioned to operate as a standalone deployment or a federated platform; in the latter case, AR researchers can contribute physical resources, including scene setup and capturing devices, and allow others to time share these resources. Our design centers around the generic sensing-understanding-rendering pipeline and is driven by the evaluation limitations observed in recent AR systems papers. We demonstrate the feasibility of this vision with a preliminary prototype and our preliminary evaluations suggest the importance of further investigating different device capabilities to stream in 30 FPS.

core_content.novelty_claim: ExpAR is a platform providing scalable and controllable AR experimentation by centering the key design insight of generalizable AR pipelines, addressing evaluation challenges such as lack of control over physical environment, time-consuming data capturing, and difficulty reproducing baseline results.
core_content.key_methodology: ExpAR operates as a standalone deployment or a federated platform where researchers can contribute physical resources including scene setup and capturing devices. The design centers around the generic sensing-understanding-rendering pipeline to facilitate fair and scalable evaluation of AR systems.
outcomes: Demonstrates feasibility with a preliminary prototype
Suggests importance of investigating different device capabilities for 30 FPS streaming
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper introduces ExpAR, an AR experimentation platform designed to provide scalable and controllable AR evaluations. The authors address key evaluation challenges such as the lack of control over physical environments, time-consuming data capturing, and difficulty reproducing baseline results. By leveraging programmable mobility sensors and remote access mechanisms, ExpAR aims to streamline the process of collecting high-quality data for AR research. The platform is envisioned to operate both as a standalone deployment or in a federated network of sites, allowing researchers to contribute physical resources and share them with others. Preliminary evaluations suggest that further investigation into different device capabilities is necessary to achieve 30 FPS streaming.
